Executive Summary

  • ✅ Best company mix for satisfaction: Startup-Heavy yields the highest average satisfaction (0.571).
  • ❌ Recession impact: Boom→Recession reduces satisfaction from 0.556 to 0.515 (Δ=0.041) and increases turnover from 13.5% to 20.7%.
  • 📈 Gender pay parity: Mean ratio 1.000 (σ=0.015).
  • ⚖️ Pay Parity Model Note: The model uses a gender-blind compensation formula — pay is determined entirely by performance score, seniority, and role level, not by gender. Any observed pay gap emerges from differential promotion rates, tenure distributions, and random performance variation rather than direct discrimination.
Jump to full analysis ↓

1. Design of Experiments

A full-factorial design explores how company mix, economic climate, and labour market size interact to shape corporate world dynamics.

Factors

FactorLevelsDescription
Company Mix5Balanced, Startup-Heavy, Old-Economy, Consulting-Driven, Diverse
Economic Climate4Boom, Normal, Stagnation, Recession
Labour Market3Small (2k), Mid (5k), Large (10k)

Company Style Profiles

StyleKey Traits
🚀 StartupSmall, innovative, high workload, volatile
🏢 CorporateLarge, hierarchical, political, stable
🔥 SweatshopHigh workload, low recognition, toxic
⭐ GreatPlaceHigh recognition, learning-oriented, caring
📝 BureaucracyProcess-heavy, secure, low innovation
💼 ConsultancyIntense projects, high pay, up-or-out culture
🏠 FamilyBusinessRelaxed, loyal, flat structure, slow growth
🎲 RandomStochastic baseline — all parameters randomised

3. Monte Carlo Summary Dashboard

20-panel overview showing distributions, correlations, convergence, stress dynamics, burnout, and factor effects.

3.1 Scenario Distributions

Key workforce KPIs broken down by company culture mix, economic environment, and labour market conditions.

3.2 Cross-KPI Relationships

Pairwise scatter plots revealing correlations and trade-offs between satisfaction, turnover, profitability, and equity.

3.3 Heatmaps & Convergence

Interaction heatmaps showing how company mix and economy jointly affect KPIs, plus Monte Carlo convergence diagnostics.

3.4 Factor Effects & Pareto

Main-effect decomposition, brand-turnover dynamics, and the Pareto frontier of satisfaction vs. cost.

3.5 Time Series & Outcomes

Temporal evolution of stress and turnover, plus burnout incidence and median tenure across scenarios.

4. Key Insights

✅ Best company mix for satisfaction: Startup-Heavy yields the highest average satisfaction (0.571).
❌ Recession impact: Boom→Recession reduces satisfaction from 0.556 to 0.515 (Δ=0.041) and increases turnover from 13.5% to 20.7%.
📈 Gender pay parity: Mean ratio 1.000 (σ=0.015).
⚖️ Pay Parity Model Note: The model uses a gender-blind compensation formula — pay is determined entirely by performance score, seniority, and role level, not by gender. Any observed pay gap emerges from differential promotion rates, tenure distributions, and random performance variation rather than direct discrimination.
🔗 Satisfaction–Turnover link: r = -0.195.
💨 Stress–Performance trade-off: r = -0.690.

7. KPI Correlation Analysis

💡 Correlation Insights

What these workforce relationships reveal — with HR science context and recommendations.

KPI PairrStrengthActionable Insight
Performance ↔ Burnout-0.979StrongPerformance and Burnout show a negative relationship (r=-0.98). Improving one may come at the expense of the other.
Tenure ↔ Med.Tenure0.952StrongTenure and Med.Tenure show a positive relationship (r=0.95). Changes in one tends to reinforce the other.
Performance ↔ Skill0.851StrongPerformance and Skill show a positive relationship (r=0.85). Changes in one tends to reinforce the other.
Skill ↔ Employment-0.845StrongSkill and Employment show a negative relationship (r=-0.85). Improving one may come at the expense of the other.
Skill ↔ Burnout-0.755StrongSkill and Burnout show a negative relationship (r=-0.75). Improving one may come at the expense of the other.
Satisfaction ↔ Compensation0.740StrongPay increases satisfaction but with diminishing returns above market median. Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory classifies pay as a hygiene factor — its absence causes dissatisfaction, but its presence alone does not drive engagement (Herzberg, 1959).
Performance ↔ Employment-0.716StrongPerformance and Employment show a negative relationship (r=-0.72). Improving one may come at the expense of the other.
Stress ↔ Burnout0.695ModerateStress and Burnout show a positive relationship (r=0.69). Changes in one tends to reinforce the other.

9. Methodology & Model Description

9.1 Architecture

Agent-based discrete-time microsimulation with quarterly time steps. Fully vectorised (struct-of-arrays, no OOP) running inside ProcessPoolExecutor for embarrassingly parallel Monte Carlo execution.

9.2 Employee Model

15 innate personality traits (Beta(2.5,2.5)) and 19 dynamic state variables. Performance, satisfaction, and flight risk equations match MATLAB V2.0 specification exactly.

9.3 Company Model

7-dimensional culture DNA, HR policies, financial model. 8 archetype styles supported.

9.4 Monte Carlo Design

5 mixes × 4 economies × 3 markets = 60 combinations × 350 reps = 21000 runs. 15 years (60 quarters) each. Executed with 28 CPU cores.

Talent Retention ROI

Boom→Recession: satisfaction drops Δ 0.041, turnover rises 13.5% → 20.7%. The Startup-Heavy mix shows highest recession resistance.

Startup vs Corporate Trade-off

Talent War by Market Size

Cost of Toxicity

05
🌎
Economic Cycle Playbook
Boom: invest in growth (revenue ×1.25). Recession: protect core team. Startup-Heavy mix weathers downturns best.

13. Financial Impact of HR Initiatives

Quantifying the business case for engagement and retention programmes.

Cost of Turnover by Seniority

LevelEst. Replacement Cost% of Salary
Junior (Rank 1-2)40,686 €50%
Mid-Level (Rank 3-4)108,497 €100%
Senior (Rank 5-6)244,118 €150%
Executive (Rank 7-8)433,988 €200%
💰 Retention ROI: Average attrition cost 167.00 M€. A 5-pp turnover reduction saves ~8.35 M€.
📈 Engagement Impact: +0.1-pt satisfaction ≈ 1.0% lower turnover (r = -0.195).

🔄 Mobility Mechanism: Each quarter ~1.25% of employees consider internal transfers. Transfers happen when destination companies have a stronger employer brand, rewarding brand investment.

📖 KPI Glossary & Definitions

Detailed explanation of every metric used in this analysis, with industry benchmarks and authoritative sources to help all stakeholders interpret results.

KPIDefinitionWhy It MattersIndustry Benchmark
SatisfactionMean employee satisfaction (0–1 scale)Core metric for retention, engagement, and productivity. Gallup links each 0.1-pt increase to 5–10% productivity gain.Best-in-class: >0.80 (Gallup 2023)
Turnover [%]Annualized voluntary departure rateReplacement cost is 50–200% of annual salary per departure. Drives knowledge loss and hiring cost.Typical: 15–20%; tech: 13%; retail: 60% (SHRM)
Employment [%]Fraction of labour pool currently employedReflects overall labour market tightness and talent availability in the simulated economy.Full employment: >95%
Compensation [€]Mean annual pay across all employeesBenchmark against market rate. Below-market pay drives turnover; above-market pay compresses margins.Industry median ±10% is competitive
Job ChangesMean number of role/company changes per employeeHigh mobility signals either a dynamic market or low organizational loyalty. Moderate churn is healthy.2–4 over a 15-year career is typical
StressMean stress score (0–1)Chronic stress >0.7 triggers exponential burnout risk. Main driver of absenteeism and presenteeism.WHO target: <0.5 sustained
SkillMean skill level (0–1)Human capital proxy. Higher skills = higher productivity and innovation capacity.Growth rate: 2–5% per year with structured L&D
Tenure [Q]Mean employee tenure in quartersLong tenure builds institutional knowledge; very long tenure may indicate stagnation.US median: 4.1 years (≈16 Q) (BLS)
Gender Pay RatioFemale-to-male compensation ratioRatio <1.0 indicates a gender pay gap. Legal and reputational risk threshold: <0.95.EU target: >0.97 by 2030
Employer BrandAvg employer brand score (0–1)Drives talent attraction. Companies with score >0.8 attract 2× applicants at 30% lower cost.Top quartile: >0.75 (Glassdoor)
Burnout Rate [%]Fraction of workforce classified as burned outBurned-out employees are 2.6× more likely to leave and 63% more likely to take sick days.Target: <15% (WHO ICD-11)
Avg Age [yr]Mean age of the workforceAge distribution affects benefits cost, retirement planning, and innovation dynamics.Typical workforce mean: 38–42 years
RetirementsNumber of retirements during simulationDrives succession planning needs. Each retirement removes decades of institutional knowledge.Plan for 2–5% of workforce per year

📚 Sources & References

Industry standards, academic research, and data sources backing the model assumptions and benchmarks cited in this report.

#SourceUsed For
1Gallup — State of the Global Workplace 2023Employee engagement, satisfaction benchmarks, burnout prevalence
2SHRM — Human Capital Benchmarking ReportTurnover rates, cost-per-hire, retention metrics
3Herzberg, F. (1959). The Motivation to WorkTwo-Factor Theory: hygiene vs. motivation factors for job satisfaction
4Becker, G. (1964). Human CapitalHuman capital theory: skill investment, return on training
5Bakker & Demerouti (2001). J. Applied PsychologyJob Demands-Resources model: stress, burnout, and turnover
6U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics — Employee TenureNational tenure statistics by age, gender, and industry
